name: apple-app-store
description: Apple App Store regime rubric — encodes Human Interface Guidelines (HIG) conformance, In-App Purchase (IAP) compliance under App Store Review Guideline 3.1.1, App Store privacy nutrition label / privacy manifest consistency, and App Tracking Transparency (ATT) requirements under Guideline 5.1.2. Opt-in via project-config.yaml `compliance: ["apple-app-store"]`. Layer 2 of the four-layer rubric composition (base + regimes + domain + project) per ADR-079 and ADR-081.

severity_rules.pattern: Tappable control declares frame, intrinsic size, or constraint smaller than 44x44 points (e.g., UIButton, UITapGestureRecognizer target, SwiftUI tap area).
severity_rules.description: Apple HIG mandates a minimum hit area of 44x44 points for tappable controls so users can comfortably activate them with a fingertip.
severity_rules.remediation: Resize the control or expand its hit-test rect (contentEdgeInsets, .frame(minWidth: 44, minHeight: 44), or hitTest(_:with:) override) to satisfy the 44pt minimum.

severity_rules.pattern: UINavigationBar / NavigationStack used as a generic toolbar (custom titles overflowing the bar, mixed leading/trailing icon counts, hidden back button without replacement).
severity_rules.description: HIG navigation-bar guidance requires a single clear title, predictable back behavior, and limited bar-button counts. Misuse degrades the platform navigation contract.
severity_rules.remediation: Restructure custom toolbar logic into a dedicated UIToolbar / .toolbar modifier and restore standard navigation back behavior.

severity_rules.pattern: UITabBar / TabView declares fewer than 2 or more than 5 tabs, or mixes filled and outlined SF Symbol styles inconsistently across tabs.
severity_rules.description: HIG tab-bar guidance limits tab counts to 3-5 and requires icon-style consistency. Off-pattern bars confuse users and may invite Guideline 4.0 design rejections.
severity_rules.remediation: Reduce or expand the tab set to 3-5 entries and standardize on filled or outlined SF Symbols across all tabs.

severity_rules.pattern: View uses hard-coded top/bottom inset (status-bar 20pt, home-indicator 34pt) instead of safeAreaInsets / safeAreaLayoutGuide / .safeAreaInset.
severity_rules.description: Hard-coded insets do not adapt to notch / Dynamic Island / home indicator devices and produce clipped or floating content on modern hardware.
severity_rules.remediation: Replace constants with safeAreaLayoutGuide constraints, additionalSafeAreaInsets, or SwiftUI .safeAreaInset.

severity_rules.pattern: Text declared with fixed point sizes (UIFont.systemFont(ofSize: N), .font(.system(size: N))) without UIFontMetrics scaling or DynamicTypeSize support.
severity_rules.description: Fixed font sizes break Dynamic Type accessibility — users who scale text up cannot read content. HIG requires Dynamic Type support across all UI text.
severity_rules.remediation: Use UIFont.preferredFont(forTextStyle:) or .font(.body) and adoptsFlexibleScaling; gate fixed sizes through UIFontMetrics(forTextStyle:).scaledFont(for:).

severity_rules.pattern: Custom raster icons used where an equivalent SF Symbol exists (UIImage(named:) for chevron, share, trash, person, gear, etc.).
severity_rules.description: HIG recommends SF Symbols for system iconography so glyphs scale with Dynamic Type, render in dark mode, and align with platform metrics.
severity_rules.remediation: Replace UIImage(named: "chevron") with UIImage(systemName: "chevron.right") and equivalent SF Symbol identifiers.

severity_rules.pattern: Project consumes IAP / subscription state purely from on-device StoreKit response without a server-side receipt verification call (verifyReceipt or App Store Server API).
severity_rules.description: Client-only receipt trust enables jailbreak receipt-injection and revoked-purchase replay. Apple Guideline 3.1.1 and the StoreKit 2 best-practices require server-side validation.
severity_rules.remediation: Forward the receipt or JWS transaction to a server endpoint that calls /verifyReceipt or App Store Server API and authoritatively grants entitlements.

severity_rules.pattern: Code uses Original StoreKit (SKPaymentQueue, SKProductsRequest) without a documented migration plan to StoreKit 2 (Product, Transaction).
severity_rules.description: StoreKit 2 (iOS 15+) provides JWS-signed transactions and modern async APIs. Original StoreKit remains supported but new development should target StoreKit 2 to inherit signature verification.
severity_rules.remediation: Adopt StoreKit 2 Product.products(for:), Product.purchase(), and Transaction.currentEntitlements; isolate Original StoreKit behind an availability check for legacy iOS support only.

severity_rules.pattern: Subscription entitlement decision derives from product purchase event only, without consulting Transaction.currentEntitlements or expirationDate / revocation status.
severity_rules.description: Failing to honor renewal expiration, refund, or family-sharing revocation gives revoked subscribers continued access — a Guideline 3.1.1 violation and a refund-fraud vector.
severity_rules.remediation: On app launch and resume, iterate Transaction.currentEntitlements (or App Store Server notifications) and revoke local entitlements when the transaction is missing, refunded, or expired.

severity_rules.pattern: Project sells non-consumable IAP or auto-renewable subscriptions but exposes no "Restore Purchases" affordance (AppStore.sync() / SKPaymentQueue.restoreCompletedTransactions invocation behind a UI control).
severity_rules.description: Guideline 3.1.1 mandates a Restore Purchases mechanism for non-consumables and subscriptions so users on a new device or reinstall can recover entitlements without re-paying.
severity_rules.remediation: Add a clearly labeled Restore Purchases button in settings/account that calls AppStore.sync() and refreshes Transaction.currentEntitlements.

severity_rules.pattern: App contains a button, link, or webview navigation steering users to an external payment processor (Stripe Checkout, PayPal, Razorpay, Paddle, custom merchant URL) for digital goods or subscriptions.
severity_rules.description: Guideline 3.1.1 prohibits in-app calls-to-action that direct users to non-IAP purchase flows for digital content. This is the most common rejection cause and risks app pull.
severity_rules.remediation: Remove external-payment links inside the app; route digital purchases exclusively through StoreKit IAP. Guideline 3.1.1(a) external-link entitlement remains narrowly scoped — confirm eligibility before relying on it.

severity_rules.pattern: App or shipped framework lacks a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy bundle declaring NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypes and NSPrivacyTracking when the project collects analytics, identifiers, or contact data.
severity_rules.description: Apple's privacy manifest requirements (effective March 2024) require a PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy file enumerating data collection and tracking. Missing manifests trigger App Store Connect upload warnings and rejections.
severity_rules.remediation: Add PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy to the app target (and each first-party SDK) populating NSPrivacyCollectedDataTypes, NSPrivacyTracking, NSPrivacyTrackingDomains.

severity_rules.pattern: Code calls a Required Reason API (UserDefaults, fileTimestamp APIs, systemBootTime, diskSpace, activeKeyboards) without a corresponding NSPrivacyAccessedAPICategoryReasons declaration in PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy.
severity_rules.description: Apple's Required Reason API list mandates declared, approved reasons for privacy-impacting APIs. Calling one without a manifest reason triggers App Store rejection at upload.
severity_rules.remediation: Add NSPrivacyAccessedAPITypes entries with the API category and an approved NSPrivacyAccessedAPITypeReasons code (e.g., CA92.1, C617.1) for each call site.

severity_rules.pattern: App Store Connect privacy nutrition label declarations omit a data category that the codebase or third-party SDKs are observed collecting (e.g., analytics SDK collects device id while label declares no identifiers).
severity_rules.description: Inconsistency between declared nutrition labels and actual SDK collection breaches Guideline 5.1.1(v) and is one of the top rejection / pulled-app reasons. False label declarations are a privacy-policy violation.
severity_rules.remediation: Audit every analytics, advertising, and crash-reporting SDK for collected fields and update the App Store Connect nutrition label to match. Re-run audit on every dependency upgrade.

severity_rules.pattern: Third-party SDK or XCFramework dependency known to collect data (Firebase, Adjust, AppsFlyer, Branch, Sentry, Mixpanel, Bugsnag) is bundled without an embedded PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy manifest.
severity_rules.description: Apple's commonly-used SDK list requires bundled privacy manifests. Missing per-SDK manifests cause submission warnings and force the app developer to assume liability for the SDK's collection.
severity_rules.remediation: Upgrade the SDK to a version that ships PrivacyInfo.xcprivacy; if no compliant version exists, replace the SDK or document the data flow in the app's own manifest.

severity_rules.pattern: Code accesses ASIdentifierManager.advertisingIdentifier or otherwise relies on IDFA without a prior ATTrackingManager.requestTrackingAuthorization completion check.
severity_rules.description: Reading IDFA before ATT authorization breaches Guideline 5.1.2 and the App Tracking Transparency contract; IDFA is zeroed unless the user has granted tracking.
severity_rules.remediation: Gate IDFA reads behind ATTrackingManager.requestTrackingAuthorization { status in ... } and only proceed when status == .authorized.

severity_rules.pattern: App imports AppTrackingTransparency or calls ATTrackingManager but Info.plist does not declare NSUserTrackingUsageDescription with a clear explanation string.
severity_rules.description: Without NSUserTrackingUsageDescription the ATT prompt cannot present and the app crashes on the request call. The string itself must be specific per Guideline 5.1.1 — generic text is grounds for rejection.
severity_rules.remediation: Add NSUserTrackingUsageDescription to Info.plist with a concrete, user-facing reason describing what is tracked and why.

severity_rules.pattern: Code path after ATT denial (status == .denied or .restricted) continues to attempt cross-app tracking, falls back to fingerprinting, or repeatedly re-prompts the user.
severity_rules.description: ATT requires graceful handling of denial — disabling cross-app tracking entirely and not nagging the user. Repeated prompts and fingerprinting fallbacks are explicit Guideline 5.1.2 violations.
severity_rules.remediation: Branch on status: .authorized → enable tracking; .denied/.restricted/.notDetermined → disable IDFA, ad personalization, and any tracking SDKs; do not re-prompt.

severity_rules.pattern: Code aggregates stable device characteristics (UIDevice identifierForVendor combined with model/locale/timezone/IP/installed-fonts) to construct a tracking signal as an ATT workaround.
severity_rules.description: Guideline 5.1.2(i) explicitly prohibits device fingerprinting as a substitute for ATT. Detection is mature on Apple's side and triggers automatic rejection plus account-level enforcement.
severity_rules.remediation: Remove fingerprinting aggregation. If you need a stable per-install id, use identifierForVendor alone with explicit user disclosure; for cross-app tracking, you must obtain ATT authorization.
